unit.name: Human evaluation is the gold standard but operationally fragile
unit.description: Explains why human evaluation remains the reference point for open-ended tasks while cataloging its practical and statistical failure modes.
unit.summary: Human evaluation is presented as the gold standard for open-ended generation and the target against which automatic metrics should be validated [ts=1980s] [ts=2016s]. But the lecture quickly shows why this 'gold standard' is difficult to use well: evaluators should score multiple axes such as fluency, coherence, common sense, grammaticality, and redundancy, and scores from different human studies are not directly comparable because the annotators and protocols differ [ts=2056s] [ts=2081s]. Yann then details the operational problems: human evaluation is slow and expensive, annotators disagree heavily even with detailed rubrics, the Alpaca Farm team only achieved 67% agreement among five trained researchers, many published human studies are not repeatable, humans only observe sampled outputs rather than the model's full recall, and crowdworker incentives often favor shortcuts such as preferring longer answers [ts=2101s] [ts=2210s] [ts=2310s] [ts=2438s].

key_points.text: Human evaluation is the gold standard for open-ended tasks and the target automatic metrics should correlate with.
Humans should often score multiple dimensions such as fluency, coherence, style, grammaticality, and redundancy.
Scores from different human-evaluation studies should not be directly compared because annotators and protocols differ.
Human evaluation is slow and expensive.
Even after detailed rubric discussion, five Alpaca Farm researchers agreed only 67% of the time.
A survey paper found that only about 5% of human evaluations were repeatable from public descriptions.
Humans mainly evaluate precision on sampled outputs, not recall over the model's whole output distribution.
Annotator incentives can reward shortcuts, such as preferring longer answers when working quickly.

But the lecture quickly shows why this 'gold standard' is difficult to use well: evaluators should score multiple axes such as fluency, coherence, common sense, grammaticality, and redundancy, and scores from different human studies are not directly comparable because the annotators and protocols differ [ts=2056s] [ts=2081s]. Yann then details the operational problems: human evaluation is slow and expensive, annotators disagree heavily even with detailed rubrics, the Alpaca Farm team only achieved 67% agreement among five trained researchers, many published human studies are not repeatable, humans only observe sampled outputs rather than the model's full recall, and crowdworker incentives often favor shortcuts such as preferring longer answers [ts=2101s] [ts=2210s] [ts=2310s] [ts=2438s].

kp_catalog.name: Human evaluation design and disagreement
kp_catalog.description: Human evaluation remains the gold standard but suffers from cost, slowness, disagreement, and protocol sensitivity.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because human evaluation design and disagreement clarifies human evaluation remains the gold standard but suffers from cost, slowness, disagreement, and protocol sensitivity, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
